event: admitted to the gynecological emergency unit
mass extending up to the umbilicus palpated
no malodorous and greyish vaginal secretions
mass 30-centimeter of the left broad ligament
contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis
bilobed air and fluid collection suggesting an abscess
abscess retroperitoneum along the left psoas muscle 9.1 × 7.4 × 7.5 cm
abscess in the Douglas pouch 7.1 × 5.1 cm
intravenous volume replacement with 2 L of Ringer Lactate
no response to intravenous volume replacement
moderately abundant non-purulent serosanguinous peritoneal fluid
no surgical wound that might explain the infection
extubated in the postoperative recovery room
transferred to the intensive care unit
Gardnerella vaginalis identified in the peritoneal fluid
Atopobium vaginae identified in the peritoneal fluid
Gardnerella vaginalis susceptible to penicillin G
Atopobium vaginae susceptible to all tested antibacterials
early postoperative peritonitis-induced septic shock caused by Gardnerella vaginalis and Atopobium vaginae
discharged from the intensive care unit
